findings.title: Explicit default port vs implicit port comparison incorrectly strips auth on same-origin redirect
findings.detail: Consider a redirect from `http://example.com:80/a` to `http://example.com/b`. Both are semantically the same origin. `urlparse('http://example.com:80/a').port` returns `80`, while `urlparse('http://example.com/b').port` returns `None`. The final condition on line 132 is `old_parsed.port != new_parsed.port` → `80 != None` → `True`, which causes auth to be stripped for a same-origin redirect. Similarly, `https://example.com:443/a` → `https://example.com/b` would also incorrectly strip. The http→https special case (lines 128-130) is safe because it checks both `(80, None)` and `(443, None)`, but the generic path on line 132 does not normalise default ports. A fix would be to normalise explicit default ports to `None` (or use a helper that maps scheme to default port) before comparing.

findings.title: Explicit port equivalents (e.g., http:80 and https:443) not normalised before comparison
findings.detail: On line 132, `old_parsed.port != new_parsed.port` compares raw port values. If the original URL is `http://example.com:80/foo` and the redirect is `https://example.com:443/bar`, the special-case on lines 128-130 checks `old_parsed.port in (80, None)` (True) AND `new_parsed.port in (443, None)` (True), so it correctly returns False. That specific combination is handled. But if the original URL is `http://example.com/foo` (port=None, default 80) and the redirect is `https://example.com:443/bar` (port=443), the special-case checks `old_parsed.port in (80, None)` (None → True) and `new_parsed.port in (443, None)` (443 → True), so it correctly returns False. This is also covered by the test on line 1605. So the logic is correct for http→https. However the symmetry concern exists for line 132's port comparison: `http://example.com:80` vs `http://example.com` would have ports `80` and `None` respectively, and 80 != None is True, so auth would be stripped even for the same effective origin! This is a real bug — redirecting from `http://example.com:80/a` to `http://example.com/b` (same host, same effective port) would incorrectly strip auth.

findings.title: Missing unit test for same-origin redirect (no stripping)
findings.detail: `test_should_strip_auth_*` tests cover host change, http downgrade, https upgrade, and port change. There is no test asserting that `should_strip_auth('http://example.com/a', 'http://example.com/b')` returns False (plain same-origin redirect). This is the hot path and should be explicitly tested.

findings.title: Missing test for same-scheme, same-host, same-port redirect (should NOT strip auth)
findings.detail: There is no unit test asserting that `should_strip_auth('http://example.com/foo', 'http://example.com/bar')` returns False (the common case of a same-origin redirect). While the code is correct for this case (hostname matches → False is NOT returned early, scheme unchanged → False, port unchanged → False, final result False), a test for this baseline would improve confidence in the function.
